news_summary: Advancements in vaccination, nutrition, parenting, and brain recovery research are significantly improving cancer prevention, dietary recommendations, long-term brain health, and stroke rehabilitation.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans les domaines de la vaccination, de la nutrition, de l'éducation parentale et de la recherche sur la récupération cérébrale contribuent à améliorer considérablement la prévention du cancer, les recommandations alimentaires, la santé cérébrale à long terme et la rééducation après un AVC.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de vacunación, nutrición, crianza de los hijos e investigación sobre la recuperación cerebral están mejorando de forma significativa la prevención del cáncer, las recomendaciones dietéticas, la salud cerebral a largo plazo y la rehabilitación tras un ictus.

articles.title: HPV vaccine brings cervical cancer deaths to near zero
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Between 2020 and 2024, not one woman between the ages of 20 and 24 in England died from cervical cancer. It was the first five-year window on record where that age group saw zero deaths, and a new study published in The Lancet now explains why: the HPV vaccine, […]
The post HPV vaccine brings cervical cancer deaths to near zero first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Between 2020 and 2024, not one woman between the ages of 20 and 24 in England died from cervical cancer. It was the first five-year window on record where that age group saw zero deaths, and a new study published in The Lancet now explains why: the HPV vaccine, introduced for school-age girls in England in 2008, is delivering on what it promised.

Children vaccinated against HPV at ages 12 and 13 have close to zero risk of dying from cervical cancer before age 30, according to research led by Professor Peter Sasieni of Queen Mary University of London. Without vaccination, the team estimates roughly 23 deaths would have occurred in that age group over the same period. Since the school vaccination program began, approximately 200 lives have been saved in England.

“It’s incredible to think that a single jab can almost eliminate a particular type of cancer,” Sasieni said.

What HPV is and how it causes cancer

Human papillomavirus, or HPV, is a large group of viruses that spread through skin-to-skin contact, most commonly sexual contact. Most people who contract it never know; the infection typically clears on its own. In some cases, certain strains trigger abnormal cell changes that can develop into cancer over years or decades. HPV is thought to cause 99 percent of cervical cancer cases, and is also linked to cancers of the anus, penis, vulva, vagina, and parts of the head and neck.

The HPV vaccine targets the strains most likely to cause those cancers. Given to children before they encounter the virus, it offers near-complete protection. In England, girls have been offered it since 2008; boys were added in 2019, helping cut transmission and protect them from HPV-related cancers as well.

Five years of zero cervical cancer deaths

In every prior five-year period on record, deaths from cervical cancer in women aged 20 to 24 in England landed somewhere between five and 27. The girls who received the HPV vaccine in 2008, when they were 12 and 13, are now in their late 20s. Between 2020 and 2024, the count was zero. The decline tracks almost exactly with who was old enough to be vaccinated when the program launched.

“As vaccinated generations grow older, we’ll see many more lives saved from cervical cancer,” Sasieni said, calling the current results “the tip of the iceberg.” Cancer Research UK, which funded the study, described the findings as an “incredible milestone.”

Vaccination rates falling short of the 90 percent target

The UK government has pledged to eliminate cervical cancer as a public health problem by 2040. The World Health Organization says 90 percent of girls need to be vaccinated by age 15 to get there. England is currently at 76 percent, and the shortfall is not evenly spread: uptake tends to be lowest in the communities that most need to be reached.

“It’s essential that the UK government and health systems urgently address this with targeted action to reach communities where uptake is the lowest,” said Michelle Mitchell, chief executive of Cancer Research UK.

Catch-up campaigns are now running through community pharmacies, and HPV self-testing kits are going out to women who haven’t come forward for cervical screening. Screening stays recommended for women ages 25 to 64 regardless of vaccination status.

For women who missed the vaccine

Alexandra Legg left school just before the HPV vaccine was introduced in England. In 2021, she was diagnosed with cervical cancer at 30, while planning her wedding. Her treatment involved removing lymph nodes from her abdomen. Surgeons preserved part of her cervix, and a year later, her daughter Ivy was born with the middle name Marvella, meaning “miracle.”

“Those nine months were so scary because I was at such risk of losing her at any point,” Legg said.

She’s since become a vocal advocate for the vaccine. “When Ivy is old enough, she’ll be first in the queue,” she added.

Cervical cancer is still the 14th most common cancer among women in the UK, with around 3,300 diagnoses a year. For women vaccinated on schedule, the risk is now close to zero. How far that extends will come down to whether vaccination rates recover, and how many more young people get reached before the next cohort ages through.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant real-world health breakthrough: the HPV vaccine has led to zero cervical cancer deaths in young women in England over a recent five-year period. This represents a major public health achievement with broad impact, supported by detailed research and data. The story focuses clearly on the vaccine's effectiveness and its potential to save many lives, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: HPV vaccine cuts cervical cancer deaths to zero in young women in England
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cervical cancer is caused mainly by the human papillomavirus (HPV), a virus spread by skin contact, often through sexual activity. The HPV vaccine, introduced in England in 2008 for girls aged 12 and 13, protects against the virus strains most likely to cause cancer.

**What happened** – Between 2020 and 2024, no women aged 20 to 24 in England died from cervical cancer for the first time on record. This matches the group that received the HPV vaccine as young teens. Researchers estimate about 200 lives have been saved since the vaccine program began.

**Impact** – This is the first time a five-year period showed zero deaths in this age group, showing the vaccine’s strong effect. Without vaccination, about 23 deaths would have been expected. This success highlights how one vaccine can nearly eliminate a deadly cancer in young women.

**What's next step** – The UK aims to eliminate cervical cancer by 2040 but needs to increase HPV vaccination rates from 76% to 90%. Efforts include catch-up vaccine campaigns and self-testing kits for cervical screening. Improving vaccine reach, especially in communities with low uptake, will save more lives.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The HPV vaccine has nearly stopped cervical cancer deaths in young women in England, proving its powerful role in preventing this disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le vaccin contre le HPV réduit à zéro les décès par cancer du col de l’utérus chez les jeunes femmes en Angleterre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer du col de l’utérus est principalement causé par le virus du papillome humain (HPV), transmis par contact cutané, souvent sexuel. Le vaccin contre le HPV, introduit en Angleterre en 2008 pour les filles de 12 à 13 ans, protège contre les souches du virus les plus à risque de cancer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Entre 2020 et 2024, aucune femme âgée de 20 à 24 ans en Angleterre n’est décédée d’un cancer du col de l’utérus, une première dans les archives. Ce groupe correspond aux jeunes filles vaccinées à l’adolescence. Les chercheurs estiment que le programme de vaccination a sauvé environ 200 vies.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’une période de cinq ans enregistre zéro décès dans cette tranche d’âge, démontrant l’efficacité du vaccin. Sans vaccination, environ 23 décès auraient été attendus. Ce succès montre qu’un seul vaccin peut presque éliminer un cancer mortel chez les jeunes femmes.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le Royaume-Uni vise à éliminer ce cancer d’ici 2040, mais doit augmenter le taux de vaccination contre le HPV de 76 % à 90 %. Des campagnes de rattrapage et des kits d’auto-dépistage sont en place. Améliorer la couverture vaccinale, surtout dans les zones à faible taux, sauvera encore plus de vies.

**En une phrase** – Le vaccin contre le HPV a presque stoppé les décès par cancer du col de l’utérus chez les jeunes femmes en Angleterre, prouvant son rôle essentiel dans la prévention de cette maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: La vacuna contra el VPH reduce a cero las muertes por cáncer de cuello uterino en mujeres jóvenes en Inglaterra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer de cuello uterino es causado principalmente por el virus del papiloma humano (VPH), que se transmite por contacto de piel, generalmente sexual. La vacuna contra el VPH, introducida en Inglaterra en 2008 para niñas de 12 y 13 años, protege contra las cepas del virus que más causan cáncer.

**Qué pasó** – Entre 2020 y 2024, ninguna mujer de 20 a 24 años en Inglaterra murió por cáncer de cuello uterino, la primera vez que ocurre esto en los registros. Este grupo corresponde a las niñas vacunadas en la adolescencia. Los investigadores estiman que el programa de vacunación ha salvado unas 200 vidas.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un periodo de cinco años registra cero muertes en este grupo de edad, mostrando la fuerte efectividad de la vacuna. Sin vacunación, se habrían esperado cerca de 23 muertes. Este logro demuestra que una sola vacuna puede casi eliminar un cáncer mortal en mujeres jóvenes.

**Próximo paso** – El Reino Unido quiere eliminar el cáncer cervical para 2040, pero necesita aumentar la tasa de vacunación del 76 % al 90 %. Se están haciendo campañas de recuperación y enviando kits de autoexploración para detección. Mejorar la vacunación especialmente en comunidades con baja cobertura salvará más vidas.

**Resumen en una frase** – La vacuna contra el VPH ha casi eliminado las muertes por cáncer cervical en mujeres jóvenes en Inglaterra, mostrando su papel clave en la prevención de esta enfermedad.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a blooming cluster of white and pale pink blossoms shaped like a protective shield surrounding a stylized, glowing cervix-shaped flower bud, set against a soft, natural background of muted greens and warm earth tones, symbolizing the protective power of the HPV vaccine eliminating cervical cancer deaths.

articles.title: Scientists say most people need more protein than current guidelines suggest
articles.summary: A new review suggests that doing more exercise and eating more protein than current minimum recommendations may help people stay stronger, sharper, and more independent as they age. The goal isn't building a beach body—it's extending healthspan and maintaining the ability to fully enjoy life for decades longer.
articles.content:
A new paper published in Frontiers in Nutrition argues that current public health recommendations for exercise and protein intake are largely aimed at preventing deficiency, not helping people achieve the best possible long-term health, independence, and quality of life. The paper tackles several fundamental questions: How much protein do people really need? What types of exercise offer the greatest benefits? And what does the latest research show?

According to author Dr. Chris Macdonald (Fellow of Lucy Cavendish College, University of Cambridge, and Director of the Better Protein Institute), a growing body of evidence suggests that people may benefit from higher levels of physical activity and protein consumption than current guidelines typically recommend.

"Public health advice often focuses on the minimum people need to avoid problems," said Dr. Macdonald. "But many people want to know what they should do to remain strong, independent, and mentally sharp throughout life."

Exercise and Healthy Aging

The paper reviews research linking regular exercise to a wide range of health benefits, including a lower risk of death, better mental health, stronger cognitive function, and greater resistance to age-related decline. The evidence also suggests that combining aerobic activities such as walking, running, or cycling with resistance training may provide especially powerful benefits.

Dr. Macdonald argues that physical activity should be viewed not only as a way to avoid disease, but also as a tool for maintaining strength, mobility, and independence throughout life.

Protein Intake Beyond Minimum Requirements

The review also takes a closer look at protein recommendations. Current UK guidelines are based primarily on preventing protein deficiency in sedentary adults. However, the paper points to newer research suggesting that physically active people, older adults, and pregnant women may benefit from significantly higher protein intake.

The review further highlights evidence that higher-protein diets can support fat loss (due to increased satiety and thermic effect), making them beneficial for body composition as well as overall health.

Importantly, the paper notes that higher protein consumption is not limited to meat-based diets. With thoughtful meal planning, plant-based diets can also provide sufficient protein, as demonstrated by the growing number of vegan powerlifters and bodybuilders.

From Minimum Health to Optimal Health

Rather than replacing existing recommendations, Dr. Macdonald suggests supplementing them with guidance focused on what he calls "optimal health outcomes." He argues that people would benefit from clearer and more practical information about how exercise and nutrition can support long-term physical and cognitive performance.

In addition to updating guidelines, Dr. Macdonald believes there needs to be a shift in how society views exercise and protein intake.

"… high-intensity exercise and high-protein diets are often associated with bodybuilders and superficial aesthetic goals. However, high-intensity exercise and high-protein diets also empower the general population to extend their lifespan and healthspan. Therefore, it is less about having 'abs' and a 'beach body' and more about being able to lift up, play with, and even remember, your grandchildren thanks to a strong and resilient body and mind. When we see a stereotypical image of a hunched-over slow, fragile person with ill health, in their later years, it seems like an inevitable consequence of "Father Time," however, I propose that in most cases, it is evidence of a non-evidence-based lifestyle. In short, we should not be quick to normalize and accept the consequences of a largely sedentary lifestyle; we should proactively empower people to reclaim their health and their independence. The reduction in unnecessary suffering would be profound."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a new scientific review suggesting that higher protein intake and more exercise than current guidelines recommend can significantly improve long-term health, cognitive function, and independence as people age. This has broad public health implications by potentially extending healthspan and reducing age-related decline, offering practical guidance for a healthier aging population.
articles.personality_title: New research suggests more protein and exercise improve health as we age
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Current health advice sets minimum protein and exercise levels mainly to prevent illness, but many people want to know how to stay strong and sharp for longer.

**What happened** – A new scientific review led by Dr. Chris Macdonald from the University of Cambridge found that higher levels of exercise and protein intake than currently recommended may help people remain healthier, more independent, and mentally clear as they get older. The review highlights the benefits of combining aerobic activities like walking or cycling with resistance training, and suggests that more protein supports muscle strength and fat loss.

**Impact** – This research shows that guidelines focused only on avoiding deficiencies miss the chance to guide people toward optimal health and longer independence. It also stresses that higher protein diets can be plant-based, making these benefits accessible to more people. The findings encourage a shift away from thinking about protein and exercise just for appearance, toward using them to maintain real-life abilities like playing with grandchildren or staying active.

**What's next step** – Experts suggest updating public health advice to include clearer, practical tips on how to use exercise and protein for long-term strength and brain health. This could lead to better support for healthy aging and reduce the physical and mental decline many experience.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating more protein and exercising more than current minimums can help people stay stronger, sharper, and more independent as they age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude recommande plus de protéines et d'exercice pour mieux vieillir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les conseils de santé actuels fixent des niveaux minimums de protéines et d'exercice surtout pour éviter les maladies, mais beaucoup veulent savoir comment rester forts et alertes plus longtemps.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle revue scientifique dirigée par le Dr Chris Macdonald de l'Université de Cambridge montre que des niveaux plus élevés d'exercice et de protéines que ceux recommandés aujourd'hui peuvent aider à rester en meilleure santé, plus indépendant et mentalement vif en vieillissant. L'étude met en avant les bienfaits d’activités comme la marche ou le vélo combinées à la musculation, et souligne que plus de protéines soutiennent la force musculaire et la perte de graisse.

**Impact** – Cette recherche révèle que les recommandations actuelles, centrées sur la simple prévention des carences, manquent une occasion d’orienter vers une santé optimale et une plus longue autonomie. Elle souligne aussi que les régimes riches en protéines peuvent être végétaux, rendant ces bienfaits accessibles à plus de monde. Ces résultats invitent à considérer protéines et exercice non pas seulement pour l’apparence, mais pour conserver des capacités réelles comme jouer avec ses petits-enfants ou rester actif.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts suggèrent de mettre à jour les conseils de santé publique pour inclure des recommandations claires et pratiques sur l’exercice et les protéines pour la force et la santé mentale à long terme. Cela pourrait mieux soutenir un vieillissement en bonne santé et réduire le déclin physique et mental.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Manger plus de protéines et faire plus d’exercice que les minimums actuels peut aider à rester plus fort, plus alerte et plus indépendant en vieillissant.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación recomienda más proteínas y ejercicio para un envejecimiento saludable
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las recomendaciones actuales de salud establecen niveles mínimos de proteínas y ejercicio principalmente para evitar enfermedades, pero muchas personas quieren saber cómo mantenerse fuertes y ágiles por más tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva revisión científica liderada por el Dr. Chris Macdonald de la Universidad de Cambridge encontró que niveles más altos de ejercicio y consumo de proteínas que los recomendados actualmente pueden ayudar a las personas a mantenerse más saludables, independientes y mentalmente claros a medida que envejecen. La revisión destaca los beneficios de combinar actividades aeróbicas como caminar o andar en bicicleta con entrenamiento de fuerza, y sugiere que más proteínas apoyan la fuerza muscular y la pérdida de grasa.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que las guías que solo buscan evitar deficiencias no aprovechan la oportunidad de guiar a las personas hacia una salud óptima y una mayor independencia. También enfatiza que las dietas altas en proteínas pueden ser basadas en plantas, haciendo estos beneficios accesibles a más personas. Los hallazgos proponen cambiar la visión de las proteínas y el ejercicio, desde un enfoque en la apariencia hacia mantener habilidades reales como jugar con los nietos o mantenerse activo.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos sugieren actualizar las recomendaciones de salud pública para incluir consejos claros y prácticos sobre cómo usar el ejercicio y las proteínas para la fuerza y la salud cerebral a largo plazo. Esto podría mejorar el apoyo para un envejecimiento saludable y reducir el deterioro físico y mental.

**Frase clave** – Comer más proteínas y hacer más ejercicio que los mínimos actuales puede ayudar a las personas a mantenerse más fuertes, ágiles e independientes al envejecer.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, vibrant oak tree with strong, thick branches shaped like muscular arms lifting a barbell made of intertwined plant-based protein sources, set against a soft natural background of a winding path symbolizing a lifelong journey of health and independence.

articles.title: Parenting may permanently improve brain health for mums and dads
articles.summary: Raising children appears to keep the brain young, potentially acting as a buffer against cognitive decline and Alzheimer’s
articles.content:
The challenges of parenting may build cognitive reserve Craig Boylan

When you are pregnant, everything changes. Hormones surge, your belly swells, cravings kick in. Until recently, the story was assumed to end there – that once the physiological and hormonal upheaval of pregnancy and birth resolves, the body and mind largely return to a pre-pregnancy state. It is clear now that this isn’t true.

During pregnancy, another transformation unfolds. Inside the skull, the brain quietly remodels itself in ways that shape a mother’s ability to care for her child. Far from being temporary, many of these changes persist for years – perhaps even a lifetime. This phenomenon isn’t restricted to the person who gives birth, either. Fathers’ brains also experience changes during the transition to parenthood. “Few brain regions go untouched,” says Emily Jacobs, a professor of neuroscience at the University of California, Santa Barbara.

Scientific consensus about the parental brain has undergone a profound shift in the past decade. What was once dismissed as “mum brain” – a forgetful, sleep-deprived state offset by an almost superhero-like vigilance for your child – is now understood as something more complex: a combination of highly orchestrated neurological adaptations that may influence everything from empathy and attention to memory and even Alzheimer’s risk.

From the earliest stages of pregnancy, grey matter – the brain tissue packed with neuronal connections – begins to change. In many regions, it shrinks as connections are pruned. Researchers stress that this isn’t a sign of damage, but of fine-tuning. “Think of Michelangelo’s David, where the underlying beauty is revealed through the art of removal,” says Jacobs.

A series of studies – including one in which Jacobs and her colleagues scanned a woman’s brain 26 times from pre-conception to two years after birth – show that the most striking transformations occur within the default mode network, a system involved in self-reflection, planning, and emotional and social cognition.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

These changes are linked with how easily a mother connects with her baby and how strongly she responds to her child’s cues – the greater the changes, the greater the bond. “The brain is becoming more specialised, rather than impaired,” says Lauren Mahoney, a psychologist at City University of New York. “[It] appears to prioritise information that is relevant to caregiving, threat detection, emotional interpretation and rapid environmental monitoring.” New mums will recognise this phenomenon – while they may forget where they placed their keys, they are remarkably attuned to subtle changes in their baby’s breathing or demeanour.

Jacobs and her colleagues are now studying the brains of other first- and second-time mums, alongside fathers and those who have never been pregnant. In unpublished work, they found that 97 per cent of the 400 brain regions examined changed significantly during a first pregnancy. Second-time mums, meanwhile, showed less dramatic shifts, having only partially rebounded in the postpartum period.

These discoveries are updating the way we think about the transition to motherhood, says Jacobs. “It’s dispelling outdated notions of ‘mummy brain’ as being dysfunctional or inadequate, and instead portraying the maternal brain as one capable of continual adaptation.”

Other evidence indicates that parenthood alters fathers’ brains, too. Like mothers, they experience reductions in grey matter volume after the birth of a child. These changes are also associated with more sensitive caregiving behaviours, such as resourcefulness and affectionate touch. In fact, the more time spent directly caring for children, the more fathers’ brain activity resembles that seen in pregnant women and new mothers.

Parenthood also seems to change fathers’ brains GFC Collection/Alamy

It is important to note that most of these studies included only heterosexual couples or didn’t ask people about their gender identity or sexuality. So it is unclear whether same-sex couples or non-binary individuals experience the same brain changes.

It is also unclear how long these changes last. But evidence suggests they endure. A study in 2021 showed that pregnancy-related reductions in grey matter were still present six years after birth. In another study, neuroscientist Edwina Orchard at the Ann S. Bowers Women’s Brain Health Initiative in California and her colleagues showed that several brain regions remodelled in pregnancy and early parenthood continued to differ between parents and non-parents in their 70s, indicating some changes may last a lifetime.

The parental brain also seems to come with durable improvements in cognition. Compared with women without children, mothers have superior attention and “executive functioning” for at least three years after birth. Executive function acts as the brain’s air traffic control system – it enables us to solve problems, switch between tasks and prioritise important information while tuning out distractions. This may come as little surprise to anyone who has watched a parent simultaneously cook dinner, stop a child from scribbling on the wall and locate a missing shoe without a pause.

A buffer against ageing

The very challenges of parenting may benefit brain health in later life by building “cognitive reserve” – the brain’s resilience to damage, illness or cognitive decline.

The idea is rooted in the blunt fact that parenting is hard. It involves taking on more responsibilities, learning a constant stream of novel skills and juggling multiple priorities alongside existing responsibilities. Add in sleep deprivation and limited resources, and parenting equates to a formidable cognitive challenge.

Over time, this sustained mental workout may act much like learning a second language or playing a musical instrument – building larger, more flexible and efficient neural networks that help ward off dementia.

Demonstrating this in humans is difficult, however, because factors such as genetics, socioeconomic status, diet and other lifestyle choices intersect with parenthood to influence brain health.

That said, intriguing patterns have emerged. For instance, in the only study of late-life maternal brain activity, Orchard and her colleagues compared patterns of brain activity among adults in their 70s with models of age-related decline. They found that mothers with more children showed patterns associated with younger brains. Orchard suggests the results are the first indication that the challenges of parenting may contribute to a mother’s lifelong cognitive reserve.

Parenting is like a sustained workout for the brain plainpicture/Lassalle, Bénédicte

Similarly, a 2025 study of nearly 28,000 people, which Orchard also led, found that both mothers and fathers have younger-looking brains in mid- to late-life than those without children. Given the effect is seen across sexes, it suggests that parenthood, not just pregnancy, shapes the brain, potentially for the better.

While there are nuances to consider – genetics might make someone more fertile and more likely to experience brain changes, for instance – it does raise the intriguing possibility that parenthood helps buffer brains against conditions like Alzheimer’s.

Most research points to a U-shaped relationship between the number of children you have and dementia risk, says Mieke Thomeer, a sociologist at the University of Alabama at Birmingham. The greatest risks are associated with having no children or having lots, typically four or more. But other studies show conflicting results.

The inconsistencies come partly from differences in how cognitive decline is defined and the types of populations studied, says Thomeer. When she carried out a study attempting to control for these factors, many of the associations disappeared. “In other words, it seems that there are multiple childhood and adolescent factors that impact both whether a person has children and how many children they have, as well as their later-life cognition.”

The picture may also be changing across generations. In a second study presented at a cognitive ageing conference at Vanderbilt University in Tennessee in May, Thomeer and her colleagues showed that in more recent birth cohorts, having no children is increasingly associated with better cognitive health in later life.

Thomeer suggests that this trend may reflect changing socioeconomic factors. For instance, child-free women are now more likely to be highly educated than in the past. It could also indicate unique stressors of modern parenting.

Parenting may influence the ageing brain through biology as well. Fetal cells cross the placenta and embed themselves in the mother’s organs, including her brain, a phenomenon called microchimerism. Here, they appear capable of turning into neurons and immune cells, raising the possibility that they may play a role in brain repair. A 2012 study found women with Alzheimer’s disease have fewer male cells – presumably from sons – in their brains than women without the condition, hinting that the cells offer some protection.

So, the quest to understand the parental brain continues. Having children running around the house is no guaranteed defence against dementia, nor is it a temporary biological event that leaves us untouched. It boosts our empathy, multitasking skills and perhaps even our cognitive reserve. Whether that wards off cognitive decline in our later years remains to be seen. One thing is clear: children don’t just leave a lasting imprint on our homes, routines and sleep. They leave an indelible mark on the brain itself.
articles.reasoning: The article reports scientific findings that parenthood induces lasting positive brain changes in both mothers and fathers, potentially improving cognitive reserve and reducing risk of cognitive decline and Alzheimer's. This has broad significance for public understanding of brain health and aging, with substantial context and evidence provided.
articles.personality_title: Parenting may keep the brain younger and sharper for years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists used to think that pregnancy and early parenthood only caused temporary changes in the brain. But new research shows that having children can lead to lasting changes in the brains of both mothers and fathers.

**What happened** – Studies found that during pregnancy and after birth, parts of the brain involved in caring, planning, and understanding emotions change shape and function. These changes help parents become more attentive and better at handling their child’s needs. Fathers’ brains also change when they spend time caring for their children. Some brain changes can last for many years, even decades.

**Impact** – These brain changes may improve parents’ ability to focus, solve problems, and respond quickly to their environment. Researchers believe that the mental challenges of raising children act like a workout for the brain, helping it stay healthy and possibly lowering the risk of memory loss and diseases like Alzheimer’s later in life.

**What's next step** – Scientists are continuing to study how long these brain changes last and whether they protect against brain diseases as parents get older. They also want to learn if these effects are the same for all kinds of families, including same-sex parents.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Becoming a parent changes the brain in ways that may help it stay strong and healthy for many years.
articles.personality_title_fr: La parentalité pourrait garder le cerveau plus jeune et plus vif pendant des années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient autrefois que la grossesse et la petite enfance ne provoquaient que des changements temporaires dans le cerveau. Mais de nouvelles recherches montrent que le fait d’avoir des enfants peut entraîner des changements durables dans le cerveau des mères et des pères.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des études ont révélé que pendant la grossesse et après la naissance, certaines parties du cerveau liées aux soins, à la planification et à la compréhension des émotions changent de forme et de fonction. Ces changements aident les parents à être plus attentifs et à mieux répondre aux besoins de leur enfant. Le cerveau des pères change aussi lorsqu’ils passent du temps à s’occuper de leurs enfants. Certains de ces changements peuvent durer plusieurs années, voire des décennies.

**Impact** – Ces changements cérébraux peuvent améliorer la capacité des parents à se concentrer, résoudre des problèmes et réagir rapidement à leur environnement. Les chercheurs pensent que les défis mentaux liés à l’éducation des enfants agissent comme un entraînement pour le cerveau, aidant à le garder en bonne santé et réduisant peut-être le risque de perte de mémoire et de maladies comme Alzheimer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques poursuivent leurs recherches pour savoir combien de temps ces changements cérébraux durent et s’ils protègent contre les maladies du cerveau avec l’âge. Ils veulent aussi savoir si ces effets sont les mêmes pour tous les types de familles, y compris les parents de même sexe.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Devenir parent modifie le cerveau d’une manière qui pourrait l’aider à rester fort et en bonne santé pendant de nombreuses années.
articles.personality_title_es: La paternidad podría mantener el cerebro más joven y ágil durante años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Antes, los científicos pensaban que el embarazo y la crianza solo causaban cambios temporales en el cerebro. Pero nuevas investigaciones muestran que tener hijos puede provocar cambios duraderos en el cerebro de madres y padres.

**Qué pasó** – Los estudios encontraron que durante el embarazo y después del nacimiento, partes del cerebro relacionadas con el cuidado, la planificación y la comprensión de emociones cambian su forma y funcionamiento. Estos cambios ayudan a los padres a estar más atentos y responder mejor a las necesidades de sus hijos. El cerebro de los padres también cambia cuando pasan tiempo cuidando a sus hijos. Algunos de estos cambios pueden durar muchos años, incluso décadas.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios cerebrales pueden mejorar la capacidad de los padres para concentrarse, resolver problemas y reaccionar rápidamente a su entorno. Los investigadores creen que los retos mentales de criar hijos actúan como un ejercicio para el cerebro, ayudándolo a mantenerse saludable y posiblemente reduciendo el riesgo de pérdida de memoria y enfermedades como el Alzheimer.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos continúan estudiando cuánto duran estos cambios cerebrales y si protegen contra enfermedades cerebrales a medida que los padres envejecen. También quieren saber si estos efectos son iguales en todo tipo de familias, incluyendo las de parejas del mismo sexo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Ser padre o madre cambia el cerebro de formas que pueden ayudar a que se mantenga fuerte y saludable durante muchos años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined neural networks glowing softly in gentle earth tones, their connections pruning and reshaping like delicate sculpted clay, surrounded by subtle abstract shapes symbolizing attentive parent-child bonds and lifelong brain growth.

articles.title: How some people's brains make an extraordinary recovery from stroke
articles.summary: Around a third of people are able to almost fully rebuild their brains after a stroke and uncovering why is pointing the way to better treatments for everyone
articles.content:
Around a third of people are able to almost fully rebuild their brains after a stroke and uncovering why is pointing the way to better treatments for everyone

Scientists are working to enhance stroke recovery Craig Boylan

A well-known actor who had experienced a stroke was treated by stroke specialist Sandor Nardai. The actor had been left with aphasia, or an impaired ability to speak – brutal for anyone, but “probably the most devastating thing that could happen to an actor”, says Nardai.

After three months of recovery, though, the actor was able to say some words. After a year, he voiced a commercial. Remarkably, he eventually got well enough to return to live theatre, says Nardai, who is at Semmelweis University in Hungary.

For every happy story like this, though, there are many people who survive stroke but have less encouraging ones. Strokes send the brain into freefall, damaging areas that control cognitive and physical functions. Only an estimated 35 per cent of survivors make a full recovery or live with only minor impairments. The majority have profoundly life-altering issues, like aphasia, paralysis, behavioural changes or cognitive and sensory challenges. The numbers are dizzying: almost 100 million live with the after-effects of a stroke, making it one of the most common causes of disability globally.

As the actor’s story shows, the brain is capable of extraordinary transformation and restoration after a stroke, but some people reap more of those benefits than others. Now, we are learning why – and with that knowledge, developing new treatments that can help more of us recover.

Why some brains recover after a stroke

A stroke occurs when a blood vessel serving the brain either bursts or is blocked by a clot. That starves the brain of needed oxygen, killing off neurons, which can leave people with severely compromised abilities to reason, learn, communicate and move. In the aftermath, the immune system also cranks up inflammation, potentially causing further damage.

Age, prior health and the extent of stroke damage are all known to influence recovery. But there are so many variables that even a supercomputer couldn’t perfectly predict who will get better, says Pankaj Sharma, a neurologist at Royal Holloway University of London. The majority of people make their most dramatic progress within six months of their stroke. Research suggests that improvement after that point is possible, but it isn’t guaranteed – some people actually deteriorate over the long term. Quick treatment in the acute phase, such as with clot-dissolving medications, can minimise the long-term ramifications, and longer-term rehabilitation, such as speech or physical therapy, can bring continued progress. But to a large degree, the brain is the maestro of its own recovery.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

This process is poorly understood, but we are learning more about what happens. Although neurons that die off during a stroke can’t be revived, those that survive can sprout new axons and new connections between brain cells can develop. After a stroke, “new freeways form,” says Sharma, “and they go around the damaged area.”

The long-standing hypothesis is that disparate parts of the brain seem to pitch in to take over the tasks that would normally have been handled by the damaged region, like co-workers covering a shift for an absent colleague, says Argye Hillis, a stroke specialist at Johns Hopkins Medicine in Maryland. However, some research challenges that idea. A 2021 study in mice didn’t find evidence to support this “remapping” effect. When William Zeiger at the University of California, Los Angeles, and his colleagues destroyed some of the mice’s brain cells, other neurons failed to jump in and take over. Instead, the researchers posited that rehabilitation might strengthen some of the surviving neurons in the stroke-affected area, allowing them to perform their old functions.

Brain health before a stroke is an important factor influencing recovery, according to a study published last year by Céline Gillebert, a researcher at the KU Leuven Brain Institute in Belgium, a finding that has also come up in Hillis’s research. Gillebert’s team looked at more than 2000 people and found that the pre-stroke condition of the brain – defined by metrics like brain volume and the health of white matter tracts connecting brain regions – was a strong predictor of cognitive function after a stroke, sometimes even more so than the location of the stroke damage.

It could be that cognitively healthy people have more residual brainpower to spare when one part of the brain is damaged. Conversely, those with more education may have further to fall: a 2025 study found that college-educated people showed steeper drop-offs in high-level brain functioning than their peers without degrees.

Genetics is also an important part of the puzzle. In one review, Sharma connected several genetic variants – including the gene APOE4, which is also linked to the development of Alzheimer’s disease – with poorer recovery from strokes. Conversely, people with certain genetic profiles may have more pliable brains than others. People with a mutation of the CCR5 gene that is disproportionately common among Ashkenazi Jews, as well as some other people with European or West Asian ancestry, recover notably well from strokes, for example.

The lack of blood flow to the brain has a huge effect on the brain ZEPHYR/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

The future of stroke treatment

Unravelling what goes on in the brains of lucky stroke survivors, like the actor, is bringing us closer to therapies that can level the playing field. Through a combination of a healthy pre-stroke brain, effective acute treatment and continuing rehab, the actor saw an unusually positive result, says Nardai. Now, he and other researchers are investigating treatments that could help those who aren’t so fortunate.

Take the importance of the CCR5 mutation. This mutation also seems to offer protection against HIV, so a drug currently used in HIV treatment is being studied in the hope that it can duplicate the natural advantages seen among people born with the beneficial mutation. Research is ongoing, but initial results have been promising.

And last year, Naohiko Okabe at the University of California, Los Angeles, and his colleagues reported the discovery of a drug that, at least in mice, appears to bottle the effects of post-stroke rehab.

First, they studied the brains of people who had survived strokes, as well as mice manipulated to have similar impairments, to better understand the effects of physical rehabilitation. They found that successful rehab boosts gamma oscillations, a type of electrical signal that helps brain cells communicate, says Okabe.

Next, the researchers looked for drug compounds that could replicate rehab’s effects by boosting the activity of a type of neuron involved in gamma oscillations. They built upon the work of another UCLA researcher, Istvan Mody, who had developed a gamma-enhancing drug in the hope of treating Alzheimer’s disease. That drug turned out to have the desired post-stroke effects in mice. The next step is testing the drug in humans, a long road with no guaranteed payoff, says Okabe. But if it works, the medication could be an important new element of stroke care. “Not everyone can engage in rehabilitation,” says Okabe. Distilling the practice’s effects into a pill would provide a better option for those limited by symptoms or access.

Treatments that aim to augment the brain’s in-built healing abilities are another potential avenue. Some researchers want to repurpose drugs that are already widely prescribed, such as antidepressants, which may increase the availability of neurotransmitters thought to help maintain the brain’s ability to change. Existing anti-inflammatory drugs are another promising option, since brain inflammation can cause additional damage after a stroke. Researchers at the University of Manchester, UK, are studying whether two inflammation-lowering drugs, including one that is already used to treat conditions including rheumatoid arthritis, could help.

Then there are approaches that sound more like science fiction. Last year, a team at the University of Southern California and the University of Zurich, Switzerland, published a study on stem cell infusions in stroke therapy. Stem cells developed into mature neurons, repaired damage to the blood-brain barrier that helps keep out toxins and microbes, and helped reduce inflammation in mice.

Brain-computer interfaces are also picking up steam. In April, German company CorTec got a “breakthrough device designation” from the US Food and Drug Administration, a step meant to streamline the approval process. CorTec’s brain-computer interface links the brain with external technologies, such as computer systems, so a person’s thoughts can be turned into action. This not only helps restore lost ability, but may also help the brain rewire itself through practice.

CorTec’s brain-computer interface allowed its first patient, Matt Kidd, to move his once-paralysed left arm CorTec

Psychedelics are another exciting possibility. Hillis’s colleagues at Johns Hopkins are embarking on human trials of psilocybin, the psychoactive compound in magic mushrooms, in the hope of using it to spur growth in the brain. And in a study published last year, Nardai’s team showed that the psychedelic DMT seemed to prevent cell death, support brain rewiring and fight post-stroke inflammation in rats. Nardai says the drug – or maybe a non-psychoactive version of it – could be given immediately after a stroke to head off damage. “My idea,” he says, would be to “give the DMT maybe even in the ambulance” on the way to the hospital, though more research is needed.

Strokes are often devastating, but some people’s stunning neural growth and adaptation, like the actor’s, shows that recovery is possible. As we learn more about which brains recover and why, hopefully, more people will be able to benefit from such transformation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on significant scientific progress in understanding why some stroke survivors experience extraordinary brain recovery, highlighting ongoing research into treatments that could benefit many people worldwide. It focuses on a meaningful health breakthrough with broad implications for improving stroke recovery outcomes, supported by detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: New discoveries explain why some brains recover well after stroke
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, millions of people worldwide survive strokes, which happen when blood flow to the brain is blocked or bursts. Strokes can cause serious problems like difficulty speaking, moving, or thinking. While most stroke survivors face lasting challenges, about one in three can almost fully rebuild their brain functions.

**What happened** – Scientists studied why some people's brains recover better after a stroke. They found that factors like brain health before the stroke, genetics, and quick medical care all play a role. For example, a well-known actor who lost much of his speech after a stroke eventually returned to live theatre after months of recovery. Researchers also discovered that certain genes and brain connections help some people’s brains create new pathways to work around damaged areas.

**Impact** – Understanding why some brains recover so well helps doctors develop new treatments. For instance, a drug used for HIV is being tested to mimic a gene that supports recovery. Other studies found drugs that increase brain signals similar to those boosted by rehabilitation. These discoveries could help people who cannot do intense therapy. New ideas like stem cell treatments, brain-computer devices that turn thoughts into actions, and even psychedelic drugs are also being tested to improve recovery.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue testing these new treatments in humans to see if they safely help stroke survivors regain lost abilities. Some drugs are already in early trials, and new technology is moving closer to approval. If successful, these advances might make recovery easier and better for many people, even those with severe stroke effects.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Research into why some brains recover better after stroke is leading to promising new treatments that could help many survivors regain important abilities.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles découvertes expliquent pourquoi certains cerveaux récupèrent bien après un AVC
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, des millions de personnes dans le monde survivent à un AVC, qui se produit lorsque le flux sanguin vers le cerveau est bloqué ou éclate. Les AVC peuvent causer de graves problèmes comme des difficultés à parler, à bouger ou à penser. Bien que la plupart des survivants aient des séquelles durables, environ un tiers parvient à presque entièrement reconstruire les fonctions de leur cerveau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié pourquoi certains cerveaux récupèrent mieux après un AVC. Ils ont découvert que la santé du cerveau avant l’AVC, la génétique et une prise en charge rapide jouent un rôle. Par exemple, un acteur célèbre, ayant perdu presque la parole après un AVC, est finalement retourné au théâtre après plusieurs mois de récupération. Les chercheurs ont aussi trouvé que certains gènes et connexions cérébrales aident à créer de nouvelles voies pour contourner les zones endommagées.

**Impact** – Comprendre pourquoi certains cerveaux récupèrent aussi bien aide les médecins à développer de nouveaux traitements. Par exemple, un médicament utilisé contre le VIH est testé pour imiter un gène qui soutient la récupération. D’autres études ont trouvé des médicaments qui augmentent les signaux cérébraux similaires à ceux stimulés par la rééducation. Ces découvertes pourraient aider les personnes qui ne peuvent pas suivre une thérapie intensive. De nouvelles idées comme les cellules souches, les interfaces cerveau-ordinateur qui transforment la pensée en action, et même les psychédéliques sont aussi testées pour améliorer la récupération.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à tester ces nouveaux traitements chez l’humain pour vérifier s’ils aident en toute sécurité les survivants à retrouver leurs capacités perdues. Certains médicaments sont déjà en essais, et de nouvelles technologies approchent de l’autorisation. Si elles réussissent, ces avancées pourraient faciliter et améliorer la récupération pour beaucoup, même ceux ayant des séquelles graves.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La recherche sur les raisons de la meilleure récupération cérébrale après un AVC ouvre la voie à des traitements prometteurs pour aider de nombreux survivants à retrouver leurs capacités.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos descubrimientos explican por qué algunos cerebros se recuperan bien tras un accidente cerebrovascular
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, millones de personas en el mundo sobreviven a un accidente cerebrovascular, que ocurre cuando el flujo de sangre al cerebro se bloquea o se rompe. Los accidentes cerebrovasculares pueden causar problemas graves como dificultad para hablar, moverse o pensar. Aunque la mayoría de los sobrevivientes enfrentan secuelas duraderas, alrededor de un tercio logra reconstruir casi por completo las funciones de su cerebro.

**Qué sucedió** – Científicos estudiaron por qué algunos cerebros se recuperan mejor después de un accidente cerebrovascular. Descubrieron que la salud cerebral antes del accidente, la genética y una atención médica rápida juegan un papel importante. Por ejemplo, un actor famoso que perdió gran parte de su habla tras un accidente pudo volver al teatro después de meses de recuperación. Los investigadores también hallaron que ciertos genes y conexiones cerebrales ayudan a crear nuevas rutas para evitar las áreas dañadas.

**Impacto** – Entender por qué algunos cerebros se recuperan tan bien ayuda a los médicos a desarrollar nuevos tratamientos. Por ejemplo, un medicamento usado para el VIH se está probando para imitar un gen que apoya la recuperación. Otros estudios encontraron fármacos que aumentan señales cerebrales similares a las que mejora la rehabilitación. Estos hallazgos podrían ayudar a personas que no pueden hacer terapia intensiva. Nuevas ideas como tratamientos con células madre, interfaces cerebro-computadora que convierten pensamientos en acciones, e incluso drogas psicodélicas también se están estudiando para mejorar la recuperación.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán probando estos nuevos tratamientos en humanos para ver si ayudan de forma segura a los sobrevivientes a recuperar sus habilidades perdidas. Algunos medicamentos ya están en ensayos, y nuevas tecnologías están cerca de ser aprobadas. Si tienen éxito, estos avances podrían facilitar y mejorar la recuperación para muchas personas, incluso aquellas con secuelas graves.

**Resumen en una frase** – La investigación sobre por qué algunos cerebros se recuperan mejor tras un accidente cerebrovascular está llevando a tratamientos prometedores que podrían ayudar a muchos sobrevivientes a recuperar habilidades importantes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricate neural network shaped like a human brain, with vibrant, golden pathways forming new connections around a softly illuminated broken blood vessel symbol, set against a gentle gradient background of natural earth tones, conveying hope, healing, and extraordinary brain recovery after stroke.
